category_title: Students with low academic achievement engaging in reflective knowledge-building discourse
minimal_context: The student is working in a small group to build a three-dimensional mind-map using colored wires for a collaborative inquiry task.
dialogue:
TUTOR: Let's start our collaborative inquiry task by building a three-dimensional mind-map with these colored wires.

STUDENT: (Pushes the wires away) I don't want to do this.

TUTOR: What do you think you could add to the group's map?

STUDENT: Nothing. I'm not good at this and my ideas are probably wrong anyway.

TUTOR: Just keep trying to work with your group.

STUDENT: (Puts head on the desk) I don't care about this schoolwork, I'm not doing it.

options.A: Act persistently without showing anger or insulting the student in front of the group, ensuring they receive no irritated response or accusation, but instead responding with patience and restraint.
options.B: Wait until the student is back to normal, and then try to gain as much as possible from the times when they are willing to participate in the group.
options.C: Construct a knowledge wall before working on the collaborative inquiry task, augmenting the activity with both whole-class and small-group discussion.
options.D: Remain available without forcing interaction, first simply sitting near the student and then speaking quietly and gently touching them on the shoulder.
